coredata.dc:title: First principles study of edge carboxylated graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelsalam, Hazem
Elhaes, Hanan
Ibrahim, Medhat A.
coredata.dc:description: The structure stability and electronic properties of edge carboxylated hexagonal and triangular graphene quantum dots are investigated using density functional theory. The calculated binding energies show that the hexagonal clusters with armchair edges have the highest stability among all the quantum dots. The binding energy of carboxylated graphene quantum dots increases by increasing the number of carboxyl groups. Our study shows that the total dipole moment significantly increases by adding COOH with the highest value observed in triangular clusters. The edge states in triangular graphene quantum dots with zigzag edges produce completely different energy spectrum from other dots: (a) the energy gap in triangular zigzag is very small as compared to other clusters and (b) the highest occupied molecular orbital is localized at the edges which is in contrast to other clusters where it is distributed over the cluster surface. The enhanced reactivity and the controllable energy gap by shape and edge termination make graphene quantum dots ideal for various nanodevice applications such as sensors. The infrared spectra are presented to confirm the stability of the quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Density functional theory
COOH
Stability
Density of states
HOMO/LUMO

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Density of states and homo-lumo energy gap

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cutting graphene sheet into small clusters, (a) AHEX, (b) ZHEX, (c) ZTRI, and (d) ATRI.
The optimized structures of ATRI (a, b, c, d) and the optimized structure of ZTRI (e, f, g, h) without and with edge functionalization with carboxyl groups.
(a–g) The relaxed structures of AHEX and (h–n) ZHEX optimized quantum dots, without and with edge carboxylation.
The density of states of ATRI (a) and ZTRI (b) clusters without and with the addition of COOH groups at the edges of the triangle.
HOMO/LUMO distribution for ATRI and ZTRI graphene flakes.
DOS of AHEX (a) and ZHEX (b) before and after carboxylation.
HOMO/LUMO distribution for AHEX and ZHEX calculated at the B3LYB/3-21G level.
The IR spectra for ZTRI without and with carboxylation.
IR intensity of AHEXbefore and after attachment of COOH groups.
The IR spectra for ZHEX before and after attaching COOH groups.
The CC bond lengths and the binding energies of the selected GQDs functionalized with different COOH groups.
The energy gap (ΔE) and total dipole moment (TDM) for GQDs.

head.ce:title: First principles study of edge carboxylated graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Theoretical Physics, National Research Centre, Giza, 12622, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Women for Arts, Science, and Education, Ain Shams University, 11757, Cairo, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Women for Arts, Science, and Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Spectroscopy Department, National Research Centre, Giza, 12622, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The structure stability and electronic properties of edge carboxylated hexagonal and triangular graphene quantum dots are investigated using density functional theory. The calculated binding energies show that the hexagonal clusters with armchair edges have the highest stability among all the quantum dots. The binding energy of carboxylated graphene quantum dots increases by increasing the number of carboxyl groups. Our study shows that the total dipole moment significantly increases by adding COOH with the highest value observed in triangular clusters. The edge states in triangular graphene quantum dots with zigzag edges produce completely different energy spectrum from other dots: (a) the energy gap in triangular zigzag is very small as compared to other clusters and (b) the highest occupied molecular orbital is localized at the edges which is in contrast to other clusters where it is distributed over the cluster surface. The enhanced reactivity and the controllable energy gap by shape and edge termination make graphene quantum dots ideal for various nanodevice applications such as sensors. The infrared spectra are presented to confirm the stability of the quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Flake-like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention due to their unique electronic [1–5] and optical [6–10] properties. The distinguished properties of GQDs arise from the electron confinement in the finite size graphene cluster that leads to the opening of energy gap and quantization of electronic energy. Cutting graphene sheet into small clusters result in creation of GQDs with different shapes and edges. Energy gap strongly depends on shape, edges, and size of the GQDs. Moreover, new states (called edge states) appear in the low energy region that also depend on the size, shape (hexagonal and triangular), and edge termination (zigzag and armchair). Tight-binding (TB) calculations [1,2,5] show that there are two types of edge states in flakes with zigzag termination, zero energy states (ZES) that are degenerated and located exactly at the Fermi level and dispersed energy states (DES) that fill the low energy region and are symmetrically distributed around it [1,2,5,11,12]. ZES appear in triangular GQDs while DES appear in other shapes such as hexagonal and circular GQDs. Due to the inclusion of electron-electron interaction in the density functional theory (DFT) calculations, a tiny energy gap is opened between ZES [2,6,9]. In general, TB or DFT calculations confirmed the appearance of edge states in GQDs. Tuning the energy gap and edge states of GQDs paves the way toward numerous applications that are not possible for a bulk graphene [13–15].

The electronic and optical properties can be tuned by chemical functionalization of the GQDs. Al-Aqtash's and Vasiliev's investigations on the electronic properties and geometrical structure of carboxylated graphene have reported significant changes in the structure of GQDs after the attachment of the COOH group to the surface [16]. Mandal et al. have demonstrated that the nanohybrid GQD-porphyrin can be a potential candidate for application in solar cell [17]. Chen et al. have reported that the photoluminescence properties of GQDs can be tuned precisely by attaching chemical functionalities [18]. Moreover, different functional groups attached to the edge of hexagonal GQDs with zigzag termination have been studied by Y. Li et al. [19]. The authors provide a comparison between the effect of different functional groups on tuning the electronic and optical properties.

On the other hand, molecular modeling with different level of theory is an effective tool of studying the physical, chemical, and biological properties of carbon nanomaterials. It could be utilized for some of graphene-derivatives such as fullerene to test the functionality of its surface for gas sensing applications [20–22]. Molecular modeling could be also applied to study the effect of surface modification and biological activity for applications as anti-protease inhibitor [23,24]. Recently, the different functionalities of carbon nanomaterials have been reviewed [25]. It is noticed that substitution could affect the biological behavior [26] and electronic properties of fullerene [27,28].

It is clear that carbon nanomaterials could be directed toward certain application with substitution and/or functionalization with certain functional group. Based upon the above considerations, the present work is conducted to investigate the structure stability and electronic properties of edge carboxylated GQDs. To the best of our knowledge no work has been done on edge functionalization of graphene quantum dots of hexagonal and triangular shapes with armchair and zigzag terminations. We study the effect of attaching different carboxyl groups to the edges of the GQDs on electronic density of states, energy gap, total dipole moment, and infrared (IR) spectra.

The graphene lattice is made of carbon atoms arranged in hexagonal shape as shown in Fig. 1 . The lattice vectors can be expressed as:
a 1
=
a 2
(
3 ,
3
)
and
a 2
=
a 2
( 3 , −
3
)
,
with
a = 1.42
Å
being the carbon-carbon (CC) bond length. The arbitrary lattice-translation vector is defined by two integer numbers (n, m) as:
L = n
a 1
+ m
a 2
.

The lattice translation vector is then used to create the graphene sheet from which we will cut the desired clusters. This is a general approach that has been widely applied to define the various nanostructures based on 2D materials such as graphene [29] or phosphorene [30]. According to the direction of cutting we can define the edge termination of the clusters, hexagonal and triangular structure with armchair (Fig. 1 a, d) and zigzag (Fig. 1 b, c) terminations. The cutting of graphene sheet does not always produce structures with perfect armchair or zigzag terminations and some atoms appear on the edges with single bond to the structure. These atoms are removed from the structure to obtain the desired edge termination. The edges of all the clusters are passivated with hydrogen atoms. Throughout the paper the triangular graphene quantum dot with armchair termination is abbreviated to “ATRI”, the triangular with zigzag termination “ZTRI”, the hexagonal with armchair termination to “AHEX”, the hexagonal with zigzag termination to “ZHEX”, and when discussing in general all quantum dots we use GQDs.

The first principles calculations based on DFT [31,32] have been performed to study electronic properties of the selected GQDs. The DFT is used as implemented in Gaussian 09 [33] that utilizes a basis set of Gaussian type orbital functions. The Becke-three-parameters-Lee-Yang-Parr hybrid functional (B3LYP) [34] is employed in our calculations since it provides a good representation of the electronic structure of graphene-derivatives [35]. The structures of GQDs were fully optimized using the basis set 3-21G [36]. DFT calculations were performed on pyrene (C16H10) using the basis sets 3-21G and 6-311G∗∗to justify the sufficiency of the 3-21G basis set for both result accuracy and computational efficiency. It was found that the energy gaps of pyrene are 3.93 and 3.85eV for used 3-21G and 6-311G∗∗ basis sets, respectively. The calculation times are10 min and 1.36 h, respectively. Therefore, we consider the basis set 3-21G as an acceptable set for calculations when taking into account both results accuracy and computation efficiency [37,38].

The graphene clusters are modified by attaching a few carboxyl groups to the edges. As was stated earlier, carboxyl group is one of the most important functional groups in chemistry and biology owing to its high dipole moment. The presence of COOH group in a given structure enhances its reactivity that is needed in many applications [39]. The following parts present a study of the effect of COOH group on the stability and electronic properties of the selected clusters.

The optimal CCOOH distances (dcx) for different clusters are also presented in Table 1. The distances between the attached COOH and different GQDs are comparable to each other with lowest distance 1.48 Å in ATRI and highest 1.50 Å in ZTRI. All the optimized structures were used to calculate the frequencies. It is found that for all the clusters the frequencies are real (positive) which confirm the stability of the selected systems (see sections 4). In addition, the binding energy (Eb) of GDQs is calculated to compare the stabilities of different shapes, which it is calculated from: Eb= (NCEC + NHEH − EC-H)/N. With NC, NH, and N are the numbers of carbon, hydrogen, and carbon plus hydrogen atoms, respectively. EC and EH are the ground state energies of the isolated carbon and hydrogen atoms calculated using the same method. EC-H is the ground state energy of the GQDs. The binding energies per atom for AHEX (C42H18) and ZTRI (C46H18) are almost the same, Eb = 7.05 (eV) for AHEX and Eb = 7.12 (eV) for ZTRI, because they have similar numbers of carbon and hydrogen atoms. However, for the structures having almost the same number of C-atoms but different number of H- atoms, such as ZHEX (C54H18) and ATRI (C60H24), the calculated binding energies are 7.4 and 7.2 eV, respectively. The ZHEX (H18) requires fewer hydrogen atoms for passivation than ATRI (H24), therefore it has higher binding energy.

The binding energy of carboxylated GQDs is calculated as the difference between the ground state energy of the compound system and the sum of the ground state energies of GQDs and COOH groups. All the calculated Eb, given in Table 1, are positive which provides the stability of the carboxylated structures. Also, the binding energy significantly increases by increasing the number of attached COOH to the GQDs edges. The highest stability is observed in carboxylated AHEX, namely Eb = 2.5 (eV) for AHEX + 6 COOH.

The total dipole moment (TDM) was calculated for all the GQDs. It is observed (see Table 2 ) that for non functionalized ZTRI and ATRI it has a finite value while for ZHEX and AHEX it has zero value. This can be explained by the fact that the carbon atom attracts electron density from H atom making it positively charged with respect to the negatively charged carbon atom. Therefore, the GQDs have permanent polarization and the TDM is determined from the summation over all local dipoles at the edges. Consequently, the TDM depends on the geometrical shape of the cluster. The local dipoles from one of the three sides of the triangular cluster generate a non zero total dipole moment. While the local dipoles from the six sides of the hexagonal cluster cancel each other. Attachment of COOH groups to different GQDs significantly increases the total dipole moment, see Table 2. Triangular structures have the highest TDM ∼4 (Debye) because they have an original none zero TDM.
The increase of the dipole moment with increasing the number of COOH is not systematic, i.e. for ATRI (ZTRI) the highest value of TDM obtained by attaching 2 COOH (1 COOH) to its corners.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density of states and homo-lumo energy gap
ce:section.ce:para:
Density of states (DOS) has been calculated for ATRI, ZTRI, AHEX, and ZHEX before and after edge-functionalization. Each electronic state was represented by Gaussian function
1
2 π
α
exp
[
−
(
ε −
ε i
)
2
2
α 2
]
with broadening
α = 0.02
eV
. The Fermi energy is seated at zero by defining EF= (EHOMO + ELUMO)/2. Where EHOMO is the energy of the highest occupied molecular orbit (HOMO) and ELUMO is the energy of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbit. Fig. 4 (a) shows the DOS of ATRI functionalized with COOH at the corners of the triangle. The effect of attaching COOH to the ATRI flake is the appearance of new peaks in the spectrum. The peak shown in Fig. 4 (a) (under the arrow) is a result of the addition of COOH groups and its intensity increases by increasing the number of carboxyl group. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap slightly decreases by the addition of COOH groups, Eg decreases from 3.3 to 3.1eV as given in Table 2. Fig. 4 (b) presents the DOS of the carboxylated ZTRI, in contrast to ATRI, the energy gap is very small.

Fig. 5 shows the HOMO and LUMO for ATRI (Fig. 5 (a)) and ZTRI (Fig. 5(b)) graphene clusters. It is observed for ATRI that the HOMO and LUMO states are delocalized and distribute over the surface and edges of the cluster. The effect of addition of carboxyl groups to the ATRI is only the redistribution of the HOMO and the LUMO. This distribution illustrates that the HOMO and LUMO in ATRI are not edge states. The edge effect can be found in ZTRI, where HOMO is distributed over the edges and LUMO is distributed over the center of the flake. Therefore, these edge states in ZTRI that fill the low energy region are the reason for the small energy gap in triangular graphene with zigzag termination. The densities of states for AHEX and ZHEX before and after adding carboxyl groups are shown in Fig. 6 . Similar to triangular structures, new peaks in the DOS spectrum emerge due to the addition of COOH groups where their number and intensities depend on the number of the attached groups. A fluctuation in the energy gap (Eg) is observed as a function of the number of attached carboxyl group with overall slight decrease in Eg (in the range from Eg = 3.7 to 3.4 eV for AHEX and Eg = 2.9 to 2.6 eV for ZHEX as seen in Table 2).

The energy gap strongly depends on the shape and edge termination of the graphene flake, AHEX has a considerably higher energy gap than the ZHEX. Also, it has been already reported that Eg decreases in GQDs by increasing its size [3,40], therefore we do not discuss the dependence on size in the present work.

The HOMO/LUMO for AHEX and ZHEX are shown in Fig. 7 . The plot shows the calculations for AHEX and ZHEX without attachment of COOH and with attachment of only three, five, and six groups. Again, as observed in ATRI, the HOMO/LUMO are distributed over the surface and the edges of the structure. Therefore, for ATRI, AHEX, and ZHEX the HOMO and LUMO are bulk delocalized states. While for ZTRI, the HOMO is edge localized state that exist on the edges and the LUMO distributes over the center of the cluster.

The B3LYB/3-21G calculated IR spectra are indicated in Figs. 8–11
for ATRI, ZTRI, AHEX, and ZHEX, respectively, before and after attaching carboxyl groups to the edges. The aim of employing the vibrational spectra is to confirm that the above calculated electronic properlies are carried out upon optimized structures. The figures indicate that there are no negative frequencies, accordingly the data given in the previous sections are confirmed with the obtained positive frequencies.

Using density functional theory, the structure stability and electronic properties have been investigated for graphene quantum dots functionalized with carboxyl groups. The calculations have been carried out for hexagonal and triangular GQDs with armchair and zigzag terminations. The positive values of the obtained binding energies for all the selected quantum dots confirm their stability. The total dipole moments for hexagonal clusters are zero while for triangular ones they have finite values. This occurs due to the shape effect where for the hexagonal shapes the local dipoles at the six sides cancel each other, whereas for the triangular clusters there is a net dipole moment coming from the local dipoles at one of the three sides.

The stability of the carboxylated clusters increases by increasing the number of carboxyl groups. The attachment of COOH group significantly increases the total dipole moment with very high values observed in triangular structures, the ATRI (4.033 Debye). The increase in the total dipole moment reflects the fact that the given structures are highly reactive with their surrounding media. The edge states appear in triangular GQDs with zigzag edges are the reason for the completely different energy spectrum from that in ATRI, AHEX, and ZHEX. The HOMO in ZTRI is localized in the edges which is different from other clusters where their HOMO orbitals are delocalized and distributed over the surface. Also, the ZTRI energy gap is significantly smaller than in other dots. The HOMO/LUMO band gap slightly increases in ZTRI by carboxylation while in other clusters it decreases. It could be concluded that, carboxyl group significantly enhances the studied properties of GQDs by increasing their reactivity which dedicate its surface in many applications such as sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to Prof. A. Fakhry for a careful reading of the manuscript and the valuable corrections.
